Loss of BAX reduces apoptosis, which can lead to accumulation of cells that would normally be eliminated, contributing to tumorigenesis and autoimmune disease over time. BUB1B encodes BUBR1 (also known as MAD3L), a key spindle assembly checkpoint (SAC) kinase. Homozygous loss of BUB1B in mice leads to severe mitotic defects, massive chromosomal instability, and aneuploidy. Studies have shown that Bub1b homozygous knockout mice die very early in embryonic development

BUB3 is a spindle assembly checkpoint (SAC) gene. In mice, complete homozygous knockout of BUB3 is embryonic lethal — BUB3-null embryos die early in development due to massive chromosome missegregation and mitotic failure. This constitutes a severe impairment of survival (premature death).  Answer:

ERCC1 is a key component of the nucleotide excision repair (NER) pathway. ERCC1-deficient mice are well-established in the literature to exhibit accelerated aging phenotypes and significantly shortened lifespans. Homozygous ERCC1 knockout mice typically die within 3-4 weeks of age due to liver failu

ERCC2 (also known as XPD) encodes a DNA helicase that is a component of the TFIIH complex, involved in nucleotide excision repair (NER). In humans, mutations in ERCC2 cause xeroderma pigmentosum, Cockayne syndrome, or trichothiodystrophy, all associated with accelerated aging and reduced lifespan.

FXN encodes frataxin, a mitochondrial protein essential for iron-sulfur cluster assembly. In humans, loss of frataxin causes Friedreich's ataxia, a severe progressive disease. HTT (Huntingtin) is essential for embryonic development. Complete knockout of HTT in mice results in embryonic lethality around embryonic day 7.5-8.5 due to defects in gastrulation. Homozygous Htt knockout mice do not survive to birth, let alone to adulthood.  Answer: A

IGF1 knockout mice are viable and fertile. They are small (dwarfism) but do not die prematurely — in fact, reduced IGF1 signaling is associated with extended lifespan in mice. IRS2 (insulin receptor substrate 2) is a key signaling molecule in the insulin/IGF-1 pathway. Homozygous IRS2 knockout mice develop type 2 diabetes due to pancreatic beta-cell failure combined with peripheral insulin resistance. PLAU encodes urokinase-type plasminogen activator (uPA). Mice with homozygous knockout of PLAU (uPA−/−) are viable and fertile. PPARG (PPARγ) homozygous knockout mice die during embryonic development (around E10) due to placental defects (failure of trophoblast differentiation). This constitutes premature death/impaired survival.  Answer: A

PPM1D (also known as Wip1) is a phosphatase that negatively regulates the p53 pathway and DNA damage response. PPM1D knockout mice have been studied and are viable and fertile. PTEN is a tumor suppressor gene. Homozygous PTEN knockout in mice is embryonic lethal (mice die around embryonic day 6.5-9.5 due to defects in gastrulation and other developmental processes). Therefore, homozygous PTEN knockout mice do not survive to adulthood — they die prematurely (before birth).

RICTOR is an essential component of mTORC2. Homozygous knockout of RICTOR in mice is embryonic lethal — Rictor-null mice die during embryogenesis (around E10.5-E11.5) due to defects in development. This constitutes a severe impairment of survival (premature death).  Answer: A
